one_line_takeaway: Using a novel ensemble-transferability metric (JS_delta), the authors show adversarial examples split into feature-exploiting attacks and non-transferring 'adversarial bugs' tied to loss-landscape sharpness, which reframes both SAM's robustness benefit (targets bugs, not features, contra prior claims) and the adversarial-training-vs-robust-dataset gap (non-robust features re-emerge, not unaddressed bugs) as sharpness/feature-distillation phenomena rather than open mysteries.
key_finding: At small perturbation budgets, a substantial fraction of successful adversarial samples are 'adversarial bugs' (non-transferring to the ensemble) rather than data-feature manipulations; as epsilon increases past a threshold, attackers shift to predominantly exploiting non-robust features (e.g., for standard CIFAR-10 ResNet50, 23.1% of samples have JS_delta<0.01 at epsilon=1/255 but this drops to 0.0% at epsilon=8/255, Table 2). Adversarial training raises the perturbation budget needed to create both bugs and feature-based samples and gives broad protection against adversarial bugs specifically (Table 3). SAM training (Table 4) sharply reduces adversarial bugs (e.g., only 0.5% JS_delta<0.01 at epsilon=1/255 vs. 23.1% for SGD) but Table 5 shows SAM does NOT increase the epsilon needed to manipulate data features (||eps_f||_2 = 0.38+/-0.19 for SAM vs. 0.38+/-0.21 for standard training, both far below adversarially trained models' 1.61+/-0.81) — contradicting Zhang et al. (ICML 2024)'s hypothesis that SAM's robustness benefit comes from encouraging robust-feature learning; instead the authors attribute SAM's benefit specifically to reducing loss-landscape sharpness, which targets adversarial bugs rather than non-robust features. For robust datasets (D_R), the well-known robustness gap versus adversarial training (61.6%->2.0% robust accuracy at epsilon=8/255, reproduced in Table 7) is shown NOT to stem from residual susceptibility to adversarial bugs: at epsilon=8/255 most successful attacks on the D_R-trained model still exploit non-robust features (Table 6), and the second-order dataset D_R' (built from this model) visually resembles D_NR (non-robust) far more than D_R, indicating non-robust features have re-emerged in models trained on the 'robust' dataset. The authors argue this refutes Li et al. (NeurIPS 2023)'s claim that the robustness gap reflects an inherent inadequacy of robust features, attributing it instead to the difficulty of fully distilling robust features into a static dataset via standard training.
core_contribution: The paper argues that adversarial evasion samples arise from two distinct mechanisms rather than one: (1) manipulation of 'non-robust features' (predictive-but-brittle data features, per Ilyas et al. 2019) and (2) what the authors newly term 'adversarial bugs' — instance-specific blind spots of a particular trained model that do not involve manipulating any statistically meaningful data feature. To distinguish these empirically, the authors propose a new ensemble-based metric, JS_delta, which computes the Jensen-Shannon distance between the outputs of an ensemble of independently-seeded model instances (approximating E_theta[f_theta]) on a source input versus its adversarial counterpart, normalized by the expected JS distance between clean same-label-pair inputs. A large JS_delta indicates the adversarial sample changed the input's data features (transfers to the ensemble); a JS_delta near 0 indicates a model-specific bug (does not transfer). The authors validate JS_delta against two properties borrowed from the non-robust-feature literature (Generalization and Invariance to training-time augmentations), then use it to: (a) characterize the composition of PGD-100 adversarial samples across attack strength for standard and adversarially trained ResNet50/CIFAR-10 and ResNet18/SVHN models, (b) re-examine the role of Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) in adversarial robustness, and (c) re-examine why models trained on Ilyas et al.'s 'robust datasets' (D_R) show a persistent robustness gap versus adversarially trained models.
threat_model: White-box, untargeted-capable but experimentally targeted, l-infinity-constrained PGD (Madry et al.) evasion attacker against image classifiers (ResNet50 on CIFAR-10, ResNet18 on SVHN), using PGD-100 with step size epsilon/10, plus a fast minimum-norm (FMN) attack variant that uses ensemble gradients for evaluating a target model. This is not a paper proposing a new attack or defense; the 'threat model' is the standard PGD evasion setting used purely as an analysis testbed to decompose why adversarial examples succeed.
limitations: The paper explicitly notes: (1) adversarial bugs and feature-based samples likely lie on a continuous spectrum, not a clean binary, and even loss functions explicitly targeting bugs (Eq. 6) may still leak non-robust-feature usage; (2) Assumption 3.2 (that different random seeds converge to similar decision boundaries, justifying the ensemble proxy) may not hold for architectures/datasets with high training-run variance; (3) the invariance-based alternative metric requires augmentations during training and is sensitive to augmentation strength; (4) experiments are limited to CIFAR-10/ResNet50 and SVHN/ResNet18 (SVHN results relegated to appendix), a relatively small-scale, low-resolution image-classification setting; only PGD/FMN attacks and one SAM configuration per dataset are studied. No natural-language, multimodal, or large-scale ImageNet-style evaluation is presented.

datasets_used: Ilyas et al. non-robust dataset D_NR
Ilyas et al. robust dataset D_R
Ilyas et al. D_rand/D_det non-robust-feature-only datasets
second-order robust dataset D_R' (constructed by the authors)
Nakkiran ensemble-adjusted D_rand_perp/D_det_perp (constructed by the authors)
quantitative_results: Table 1: vanilla PGD adversarial samples retain target label 95.1% of the time under training augmentations vs. only 3.9% for highly non-transferable (Nakkiran-style) samples, which revert to source label 85.7% of the time
Table 2 (standard ResNet50/CIFAR-10): % samples with JS_delta<0.01 falls from 23.1% at epsilon=1/255 to 0.0% at epsilon=8/255, while robust accuracy falls from 66.5% to 0.7%
Table 3 (adversarially trained ResNet50/CIFAR-10): larger epsilon (up to 32/255) needed to produce either bug or feature-based samples; robust accuracy 78.8% at 5/255 down to 1.4% at 32/255
Table 4 (SAM rho=0.3, CIFAR-10): % samples with JS_delta<0.01 drops to 0.5% at epsilon=1/255 (vs 23.1% for SGD-trained), indicating strong bug protection

Table 5: minimum perturbation to manipulate data features (||eps_f||_2) is 0.38+/-0.19 for SAM vs 0.38+/-0.21 for standard training vs 1.61+/-0.81 for adversarially trained models on CIFAR-10 (analogous pattern on SVHN: 0.59+/-0.41 SAM vs 0.65+/-0.44 standard vs 1.03+/-0.64 adversarial)
Table 6 (robust-dataset-trained ResNet50): at epsilon=8/255, only 1.6% of samples have JS_delta<0.01, i.e. the vast majority of successful attacks use non-robust features despite training on D_R

Table 7 (reproduction of Ilyas et al.): standard training on D_R yields robust accuracy dropping from 61.6% (adversarially trained, Madv) to 2.0% (MR) at epsilon=8/255, confirming the known robustness gap; clean accuracy for MR is 84.5% vs 87.8% for Madv and 94.1% for standard M
Table 8: models trained on Drand and Ddet achieve 62.0% and 33.4% clean test accuracy on D respectively, confirming non-robust features alone support generalization
Table 9: ensemble-adjusted (highly non-transferable) versions of Drand/Ddet yield only 11.3% and 7.8% test accuracy, versus 62.0%/33.4% for the originals
baselines_compared: Ilyas et al. (2019) non-robust-feature theory and DNR/DR/Drand/Ddet dataset constructions
Nakkiran (2019) ensemble-adjusted attack for generating non-transferable adversarial bugs
Zhang et al. (ICML 2024) SAM-adversarial-training duality hypothesis
Li et al. (NeurIPS 2023) 'adversarial examples are not real features' robustness-gap argument
standard (SGD) vs. adversarially trained (PGD-7, epsilon=8/255) vs. SAM-trained vs. robust-dataset-trained ResNet50 (CIFAR-10) and ResNet18 (SVHN) models
